task: I’m putting together a short post-production checklist for a freelance editor before picture lock, and I want it to catch the stuff that usually slips through. Can you help me draft one that’s practical and not overkill?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical, not-overkill **pre-picture-lock post-production checklist** for a freelance editor.

## Pre-Picture Lock Checklist

### Picture / Editorial
- Confirm all notes through the latest review are addressed
- Check for any missing, duplicated, or out-of-order shots
- Verify all trims, transitions, and dissolves are intentional
- Look for flash frames, black frames, or accidental gaps
- Make sure temp inserts, placeholders, and slugs are removed or clearly marked
- Confirm all split edits and J/L cuts are clean
- Review pacing in the full sequence, not just scene by scene

### Audio
- Check for missing or mismatched temp audio
- Confirm dialogue edits are clean and no clips are muted by mistake
- Watch for pops, clicks, abrupt fades, or unwanted room tone changes
- Verify music and SFX cues are in the right spots
- Make sure any temp mixes are labeled as temp if applicable

### Graphics / Text
- Confirm lower thirds, supers, and end cards are spelled correctly
- Check on-screen text for typos, cut-off text, or safe area issues
- Verify any placeholders like “TODO” or “TEMP” have been replaced

### Color / Conform
- Check that all clips are using the correct versions
- Look for obvious color mismatches between shots
- Confirm any temp grades are labeled appropriately
- Make sure no offline or relinked media is missing

### Deliverables / Housekeeping
- Verify project file naming is clear and current
- Confirm version number is correct on exports and timeline
- Remove unused sequences or clearly separate archive materials
- Make sure reference exports match the latest cut
- Save a clean project backup before lock

### Final Pass
- Watch the cut start to finish once without stopping
- Check it on full screen and with audio on speakers/headphones
- Note anything that feels off before calling picture lock

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **one-page client-friendly checklist**
- a **more detailed editor handoff checklist**
- or a **checkbox format** you can paste into Notion/Google Docs.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty close. Useful, but a little generic in spots.

I’d want one more pass aimed at the stuff that actually bites in freelance work — versioning, timecode/frame-rate sanity, mixed-source audio, legal text, and making sure nothing temp is still living in the cut like it’s permanent. Also maybe a line for confirming what picture lock means to the client, because that gets fuzzy fast.

If you tighten it up around that, it’ll be solid.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more freelance-practical version with those pain points built in.

## Pre-Picture Lock Checklist

### 1) Confirm what “picture lock” means
- Confirm with the client what is considered locked
- Make sure they understand what changes are still allowed after lock
- Note whether lock includes titles, graphics, and legal text

### 2) Versioning / project sanity
- Confirm you’re in the latest approved cut
- Check sequence name, export name, and file names match the current version
- Make sure old versions are clearly labeled or archived
- Verify no one is working off an outdated timeline

### 3) Timecode / frame rate / technical check
- Confirm the sequence frame rate matches the delivery spec
- Check timecode start, drop/non-drop, and any broadcast requirements
- Make sure captions, graphics, and exports line up with the correct timing
- Watch for any frame-rate drift or media that was interpreted incorrectly

### 4) Editorial cleanup
- Remove or replace all temp shots, temp text, placeholders, and “to be replaced” slugs
- Check for any clips that were meant to be temporary but are still in the cut
- Make sure no guide audio, scratch VO, or temp music is carrying through as final
- Verify all transitions, trims, and split edits are intentional

### 5) Audio check
- Check for mixed-source audio issues: level jumps, tone changes, bad sync, or mismatched room tone
- Make sure dialogue is clean and no clips are muted, offline, or accidentally doubled
- Verify temp audio is clearly marked or fully replaced
- Listen for pops, clicks, clipped peaks, or abrupt fades

### 6) Graphics / legal / text
- Check all titles, lower thirds, supers, end cards, and legal text for spelling and timing
- Make sure legal copy is current and approved
- Verify text is not cut off, too small, or outside safe areas
- Confirm any placeholder text has been fully removed

### 7) Conform / media check
- Check for offline, relinked, or wrong-version media
- Make sure all source files are the intended ones
- Look for obvious color mismatches or missing media in the timeline

### 8) Final watch-through
- Watch the full cut once straight through
- Check it full screen with sound on
- Catch anything that still feels temporary, unfinished, or out of place
- Save a clean backup before sending for lock

If you want, I can also turn this into:
- a **checkbox version**
- a **shorter one-page editor handoff**
- or a **client-facing picture lock confirmation note**

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — closer. Still a bit broad in the middle though.

I’d tighten it one more notch around the stuff that actually causes freelance pain:
- version history / naming
- frame rate + timecode match to delivery spec
- mixed-source audio and any temp audio that’s pretending to be final
- legal text / approvals / spelling
- explicit client sign-off on what picture lock covers

Also worth making the “final pass” less generic. Not just watch it through — something like: one clean playback, full screen, speakers/headphones, and verify there are no temp elements left anywhere in picture or audio.

If you want, I’d rather see it as a tight checklist with maybe 10–15 bullets max, not a big sectioned outline.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter **10–15 bullet** version, aimed at the stuff that tends to cause real freelance headaches:

## Pre-Picture Lock Checklist

- Confirm with the client what “picture lock” includes: picture only, or also titles, graphics, captions, and legal text
- Make sure the latest approved cut is the one being worked on
- Check sequence, export, and file names against the current version
- Keep old versions clearly labeled and out of the way
- Verify the sequence frame rate matches the delivery spec
- Check timecode start, drop/non-drop, and any broadcast or platform requirements
- Make sure all source media is the correct version and properly relinked
- Remove or replace any temp shots, placeholders, slugs, guide text, or “to be replaced” elements
- Check that no temp audio is still being treated like final audio
- Listen for mixed-source audio issues: level jumps, bad sync, room tone changes, pops, clicks, or clipping
- Confirm all titles, lower thirds, supers, and legal text are spelled correctly and approved
- Make sure text is timed correctly and sits safely within frame
- Verify there are no offline clips, wrong-version media, or obvious conform issues
- Do one clean full-screen playback with sound on speakers or headphones
- Confirm nothing temporary is left anywhere in picture or audio before sending for lock

If you want, I can make this into:
- a **checkbox template**
- a **more editor-specific version for Premiere/Resolve/Avid**
- or a **client sign-off version** with a lock confirmation line

user_simulator.text: Yeah, that’s the shape of it. Tight enough, actually useful. The only thing I’d maybe tweak is making the client sign-off line a little more explicit, but as-is it’s solid for a freelance editor checklist.
